evidence_text: An ultralightweight Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, which contains more than 13 wt% of Al and thereby reduces the density by 20%, is developed.

section: Optimization of Process Conditions for Ultralightweight Steel with More Than 13 wt% of Al and 5 wt% of Cr
subject: Hot rolling soaking
claim: Soaking at 1100–1200 °C for 2 h before hot rolling; successful at 1100 °C, intergranular cracking at 1150 and 1200 °C
evidence_text: Hot rolling is conducted after soaking at the temperature range of 1100–1200 °C for 2 h. The ultralightweight steel is hot-rolled successfully after soaking at 1100 °C, whereas specimens soaked at 1150 and 1200 °C are intergranularly cracked after hot rolling, resulting from coarse grain and a large fraction and size of ferrite, which is transformed to ordered DO3 phase during cooling, at the grain boundaries.

section: Optimization of Process Conditions for Ultralightweight Steel with More Than 13 wt% of Al and 5 wt% of Cr
subject: Brittle phases in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels
claim: κ-carbide of (Fe, Mn)3AlC type, ferrite, and ordered phases such as B2 or DO3 at grain boundaries are brittle phases that can easily form
evidence_text: κ-carbide of (Fe, Mn)3AlC type, ferrite, and ordered phases such as B2 or DO3 at grain boundaries, which are brittle phases, can be easily formed,

section: Optimization of Process Conditions for Ultralightweight Steel with More Than 13 wt% of Al and 5 wt% of Cr
subject: Study approach
claim: Manufacture ultralightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steel with >13 wt% Al by suppressing brittle phases through Cr addition and optimization of hot rolling and heat treatment
evidence_text: In this study, it was aimed to manufacture ultralightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steel containing more than 13 wt% of Al which can reduce 20% of density by suppressing the formation of brittle coarse κ-carbide, ferrite, and ordered phases at grain boundaries through the addition of Cr and optimization of hot rolling and heat treatment conditions.

subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr hot-rolled plates
claim: Plates soaked at 1200 and 1150°C developed cracks after hot rolling, whereas the plate soaked at 1100°C was hot-rolled successfully without cracks.
evidence_text: Both plates soaked at 1200 and 1150 °C had crack after hot rolling, while the plate soaked at 1100 °C was hot-rolled successfully without cracks (Figure 1a).

subject: 1150°C soaked plate
claim: Microstructure consisted of austenite with large grain size and BCC ferrite (ordered DO3) at grain boundaries. Austenite grain size ~60 μm, DO3 area fraction 11.5%, thickness 5.5 μm.
evidence_text: the microstructure of the 1150 °C plate was austenite (face centered cubic (FCC) phase) with large grain size and ferrite (body centered cubic (BCC) phase) at grain boundaries. The BCC phase is identified as ordered DO3 phase by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, as shown in Figure 3. The austenite grain size was approximately 60 μm and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 11.5% and 5.5 μm, respectively.

subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel
claim: No coarse κ-carbide precipitates at grain boundaries despite 13.2 wt% Al, confirming that 5 wt% Cr effectively suppresses coarse κ-carbide.
evidence_text: there is no coarse κ-carbide at grain boundaries in the investigated steel in spite of 13.2 wt% of Al content. It is confirmed that 5 wt% of Cr is effective to prevent precipitation of coarse κ-carbide.

subject: 1150°C soaked plate
claim: Cracking occurred by intergranular fracture along austenite grain boundaries.
evidence_text: The crack of the 1150 °C plate was intergranular fracture along austenite grain boundaries.

subject: 1100°C soaked plate
claim: Austenite grain size 35 μm, DO3 area fraction 8.1%, thickness 2.8 μm at grain boundaries.
evidence_text: the plate soaked at 1100 °C shows relatively fine austenite grain size and small fraction and size of DO3 at grain boundaries (Figure 1c,e). The austenite grain size was 35 μm, and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 8.1% and 2.8 μm, respectively.

subject: Fe–Mn–Al–C steels
claim: Between 1100–1200°C, BCC phase fraction increases with temperature; at soaking, austenite + grain-boundary ferrite exists and transforms to ordered DO3 during cooling, leading to large DO3 at higher temperatures and vulnerability to intergranular cracking.
evidence_text: According to the calculated phase diagram of Fe–Mn–Al–C steels in the previous result reporting that BCC phase exists at the soaking temperature between 1100 and 1200 °C and the fraction of BCC phase increases with increasing the soaking temperature, it is expected that the microstructure at soaking temperature is austenite and some ferrite at grain boundaries, which is transformed to ordered DO3 during cooling. Therefore, the plate soaked at higher temperature has a large size of austenite and relatively large fraction and size of DO3 at grain boundaries, which is vulnerable to intergranular cracking.

subject: 1100°C hot-rolled plate after homogenization (1050°C/2h)
claim: Air-cooled specimen: austenite + coarse DO3 (4.2% area, 2.6 μm thick). Water-quenched specimen: uniform austenite matrix with fine κ-carbide and minimal DO3 (1.27% area, 2.4 μm thick).
evidence_text: Austenite and coarse DO3 phase at the grain boundaries were observed in the air-cooled specimen (Figure 2b,d), and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 4.2% and 2.6 μm, respectively, whereas uniform microstructure with austenite and small amount of DO3 was observed in the water-quenched specimen (Figure 2c,e), and the average area fraction and thickness of DO3 phase were 1.27% and 2.4 μm, respectively.

subject: Fe–Mn–Al–C steel homogenization
claim: Slow cooling promotes transformation of austenite to ferrite/DO3; fast cooling (water quenching) is required for a uniform microstructure.
evidence_text: It was found that transformation of austenite to ferrite (or DO3 phase) happened at a slow cooling rate, so fast cooling is necessary to have uniform microstructure by preventing ferrite transformation.

subject: κ-carbide in ultralightweight steel with 23 at% Al (13.2 wt%)
claim: κ-carbide forms rapidly without Al diffusion due to high Al concentration; water quenching cannot suppress κ-carbide formation.
evidence_text: Because the Al concentration of this ultralightweight steel was 23 at% (13.2 wt%) of Al, κ-carbide may be formed very quickly without diffusion of Al atoms, so it is easily estimated that water quenching cannot suppress κ-carbide formation in this steel.

subject: BCC (ferrite/DO3) in ultralightweight steel
claim: BCC formation requires longer time than κ-carbide because C and Mn must leave BCC and Al must diffuse to BCC; water quenching can sufficiently suppress BCC formation.
evidence_text: As C and Mn atoms should move from BCC to FCC and Al diffuses to BCC, the formation of BCC requires a longer time compared to that for κ-carbide. Therefore, the formation of BCC can be sufficiently suppressed with water quenching.

subject: water-quenched specimen
claim: XRD and TEM reveal austenite matrix containing fine κ-carbide, and BCC grain-boundary phase is ordered DO3.
evidence_text: In-depth microstructural analysis on water-quenched specimen was conducted using XRD and TEM, as shown in Figure 3, which illustrates that the water-quenched specimen has austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and BCC phase at grain boundaries was identified as ordered DO3 phase.

subject: air-cooled specimen
claim: Tensile specimen fractured in elastic region, showing no measurable yield or tensile strength, due to brittle DO3 at grain boundaries.
evidence_text: The air-cooled specimen shows very poor tensile property. Tensile specimen was fractured at elastic region and even failed to have yield and tensile strength, resulting from brittle ordered DO3 at grain boundaries.

subject: water-quenched specimen
claim: Tensile strength 1048 MPa and total elongation 38% due to uniform austenite matrix with fine κ-carbide and very small DO3 fraction.
evidence_text: the water-quenched specimen shows excellent tensile property with 1048 MPa of tensile strength and 38% of total elongation due to uniform microstructure with austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and very small fraction of DO3 phase.

subject: post-tensile fracture surfaces
claim: Air-cooled specimen: cracks initiate and propagate along DO3 grain boundaries, with no plastic deformation of austenite. Water-quenched specimen: austenite matrix significantly elongated, ductile fracture.
evidence_text: In the air-cooled specimen, the cracks initiated and propagated along DO3 phase at grain boundary and austenite matrix seems not to be plastically deformed, whereas in the water-quenched specimen, austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured ductile

subject: water-quenched specimen
claim: exhibits tensile strength of 1048 MPa and total elongation of 38% due to uniform austenite, fine κ-carbide, and minimal DO3 phase
evidence_text: the water-quenched specimen shows excellent tensile property with 1048 MPa of tensile strength and 38% of total elongation due to uniform microstructure with austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and very small fraction of DO3 phase.

subject: air-cooled specimen
claim: cracks initiated and propagated along DO3 phase at grain boundaries, with no plastic deformation of austenite matrix
evidence_text: In the air-cooled specimen, the cracks initiated and propagated along DO3 phase at grain boundary and austenite matrix seems not to be plastically deformed

subject: water-quenched specimen
claim: austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured in a ductile manner
evidence_text: austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured ductile, which are consistent with stress–strain curves in Figure 4, respectively.

subject: Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel
claim: density is 6.30 g cm⁻³, with uniform austenite, fine κ-carbide, and very small ordered DO3 fraction, yielding excellent tensile properties
evidence_text: the ultralightweight Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, density of which is $6 . { \overset { \vartriangle } { 3 0 } } \ g \ c m ^ { \stackrel { \textstyle - } { - } 3 }$, having uniform microstructure with austenite fine κ-carbide inside austenite very small fraction of ordered DO3 phase and excellent tensile property

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: Steel composition is Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr (wt%).
evidence_text: An ultralightweight steel with Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr (wt%) was designed.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: Measured density is 6.30 g cm<sup>-3</sup>.
evidence_text: The measured density of this ultralightweight steel is $6 . 3 0 \ g \mathrm { c m } ^ { - 3 }$ .

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: Al content increased to 13.2 wt% to reduce density by 20%.
evidence_text: The Al content was increased to 13.2 wt% to reduce the density by 20%.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: Mn and C were increased to 30 and 1.6 wt% as austenite stabilizers to prevent ferrite formation from high Al.
evidence_text: To prevent the formation of ferrite due to the increase in Al which is ferrite stabilizing element, Mn and C, austenite stabilizing elements, were increased to 30 and 1.6 wt%, respectively.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: 5 wt% Cr added to suppress κ-carbide formation.
evidence_text: Cr (5 wt%) was added to the Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C steel to suppress κ-carbide formation due to the increase in Al and C.

subject: Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr steel
claim: Previous study confirmed that Cr retards κ-carbide formation and slightly increases austenite fraction via C enrichment.
evidence_text: In the previous study, it was confirmed that the addition of a certain amount of Cr retards the formation of κ-carbide and thereby increased slightly fraction of austenite due to enrichment of C in austenite.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: 5 kg ingot of 30 mm thickness hot-rolled to 7 mm plate.
evidence_text: The 5 kg ingots with 30 mm thickness were hot-rolled to plates with 7 mm thickness and heat-treated for homogenization.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: Ingots soaked at 1100, 1150, 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolled above 1000 °C and air cooled.
evidence_text: The ingots were soaked at three temperatures (1100, 1150, 1200 °C) for 2 h, followed by hot rolling at a temperature higher than 1000 °C and air cooling.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel
claim: Hot-rolled plates homogenized at 1050 °C for 2 h and cooled by air cooling or water quenching.
evidence_text: The hot-rolled plates were homogenized 1050 °C for 2 h, and then cooled by air cooling and water quenching to room temperature, respectively.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel homogenization
claim: Homogenization at 1050 °C selected to dissolve κ-carbide and minimize grain coarsening.
evidence_text: Homogenization temperature, 1050 °C, was selected to dissolve κ-carbide as well as to minimize grain coarsening.

subject: Fe-30Mn-13.2Al-1.6C-5Cr steel after optimized treatment
claim: Total elongation of 38% achieved by optimizing hot rolling and heat treatment.
evidence_text: and 38% of total elongation through optimization of hot rolling and heat treatment conditions.

subject: microstructure identification
claim: Microstructure identified using SEM (JSM-7001F), EBSD (NordlysNano detector, AZTEC software), TEM (JEM-2100F), and XRD (DMAX-2500).
evidence_text: Microstructures were identified by SEM (JSM-7001F, JEOL), EBSD (NordlysNano EBSD detector and AZTEC software, Oxford), TEM (JEM-2100F, JEOL), and XRD (DMAX-2500, Rigaku).

subject: SEM sample
claim: SEM specimens mechanically polished and etched with 40% nital (40 mL HNO3 + 100 mL ethyl alcohol).
values: {'etchant': '40% nital (40 mL HNO3 + 100 mL ethyl alcohol)'}
evidence_text: Specimens for SEM were mechanically polished and etched using a 40% nital solution (40 mL HNO3 + 100 mL ethyl alcohol).

subject: EBSD sample
claim: EBSD specimens chemomechanically polished with colloidal silica without etching.
evidence_text: Specimens for EBSD were chemomechanically polished with colloidal silica without etching.

subject: XRD measurement
claim: XRD specimen prepared as block 15 mm × 12 mm × 2 mm; measurement with Cu Kα, 40 kV, 200 mA, 2θ range 20°–80°, step 0.02°, 2 s/step.
values: {'specimen_dimensions': '15 mm × 12 mm × 2 mm', 'radiation': 'Cu Kα', 'voltage': '40 kV', 'current': '200 mA', '2theta_range': '20°–80°', 'step': '0.02°', 'dwell_time': '2 s/step'}
evidence_text: XRD specimen was prepared as block with 15 mm 12 mm 2 mm after mechanical polishing. XRD measurement was performed with Cu Kα radiation, 40 kV and 200 mA. The diffraction patterns were obtained in step scan mode, 2θ = 20°–80° with a step of 0.02° and 2 s step−1.

subject: TEM sample
claim: TEM discs 3 mm diameter, mechanically polished to 80 μm, electrochemically twin-jet polished with 10% perchloric acid + 90% methyl alcohol.
values: {'disc_diameter': '3 mm', 'thickness_after_mechanical_polishing': '80 μm', 'electrolyte': '10% perchloric acid + 90% methyl alcohol', 'polishing_method': 'twin-jet electrophilishing'}
evidence_text: TEM specimens were prepared as discs with 3 mm in diameter and mechanically polished to 80 μm in thickness and then electrochemically polished in a twin-jet polishing apparatus using a solution (10% perchloric acid þ 90% methyl alcohol).

subject: tensile testing
claim: Tensile tests per ASTM E8 with subsize specimens (2 mm thick, 6 mm wide, 25 mm gauge, 100 mm total) at 2 mm/min crosshead speed, room temperature, on Instron 5882.
values: {'standard': 'ASTM E8', 'specimen_thickness': '2 mm', 'specimen_width': '6 mm', 'gauge_length': '25 mm', 'total_length': '100 mm', 'crosshead_speed': '2 mm/min', 'temperature': 'room temperature', 'machine': 'INSTRON 5882'}
evidence_text: Tensile properties were evaluated in accordance of ASTM E8. All tensile tests were conducted with subsize tensile specimens (2 mm in thickness, 6 mm in width, 25 mm in gauge length, and 100 mm in total length) at a cross head speed of 2 mm min−1 at room temperature using universal tensile test machine (INSTRON 5882, INSTRON).
